name: Bhaktivinoda Thakura

dates_evidence.description: Colonial-era Bengal civil-service records of his administrative career.

core_thesis: Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura (Kedarnāth Datta, 1838–1914) is the modern Bengali Vaiṣṇava theologian who systematized Caitanya-Gauḍīya thought in modern philosophical idiom and effectively founded the modern Gauḍīya revival. His settled position is the inherited *acintya-bhedābheda*: Bhagavān (Kṛṣṇa) is the one supreme reality, infinitely powerful (*śakti-mān*), whose manifold *śaktis* — *svarūpa*, *taṭasthā* (the *jīva*-energy), and *māyā* — produce a real world and real *jīvas* whose simultaneous identity-and-distinction with Bhagavān is *acintya* (beyond rational determination, but not contradictory) (*Tattva-Sūtra* sūtras 4–11, with auto-gloss; *Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 14–17 on the threefold *śakti*). Distinctive emphases: a layered epistemology (*pramāṇa* / *prameya* / *adhokṣaja-jñāna*) that grounds devotional knowledge as a self-standing cognitive mode beyond *pratyakṣa* and *anumāna* (*Tattva-Viveka* vv. 1–10 with auto-gloss; *Jaiva-Dharma* ch. 8 on *adhokṣaja-jñāna*); a strong doctrine of the eternal individual *jīva* whose constitutional position (*svarūpa*) is *kṛṣṇa-dāsa* (*Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 1–3, 16; *Tattva-Sūtra* sūtras 12–18); *bhakti* as both the means and the end (*sādhana-bhakti* maturing into *prema*) (*Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 18–24, the *abhidheya* and *prayojana* sections); and *rāgānuga-sādhana* under guidance of the inner *siddha-deha* (*Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 22–25). He argues, against contemporary impersonalism and Brahmo-Samāja reformism, that Caitanya's school is fully rational, fully Vedāntic, and modern (*Tattva-Viveka* vv. 1–48 with auto-gloss; *Vedānta-Adhikaraṇa-Mālā*, Introduction).

engaged_works.title: Jaiva-Dharma
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored systematic Gauḍīya doctrinal novel.
engaged_works.summary: The principal exposition of Bhaktivinoda's mature theology, a Bengali doctrinal novel in twenty-five chapters whose narrative frame (Vaiṣṇava elders in Navadvīpa instructing seekers from progressively more complex backgrounds) lets the doctrine unfold in graduated philosophical articulation. The early chapters establish *sanātana-dharma* as constituted by the eternal nature (*svarūpa*) of the *jīva* — *kṛṣṇa-dāsa* — distinguishing this *nitya-dharma* from the *naimittika-dharmas* of social-religious life (*Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 1–3 'Nitya- and Naimittika-Dharma'). The middle chapters give a complete account of the *jīva*-tattva (*taṭasthā-śakti*, the eternal individuality of selves, the structure of bondage as *vimukha-svabhāva*) (*Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 14–17), the *māyā*-tattva (chs. 16–17), and the *bhagavat-tattva* (Kṛṣṇa as the *svayaṃ-bhagavān*, with *svarūpa-śakti* analyzed into *hlādinī*, *sandhinī*, *saṃvit*) (*Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 14–15). The later chapters develop *abhidheya* (sādhana-bhakti) (*Jaiva-Dharma* chs. 18–21) and *prayojana* (prema) (chs. 22–24), culminating in *rāgānuga-sādhana* and the meditation on the *siddha-deha* (*Jaiva-Dharma* ch. 25). The work is pitched simultaneously as systematic philosophy, sectarian apologetic against impersonalism, and pastoral guide.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Sutra
engaged_works.summary: A short doctrinal sūtra-text with Bhaktivinoda's own gloss, presenting in compressed Sanskrit aphoristic form the same doctrinal architecture that the *Jaiva-Dharma* unfolds in Bengali narrative. The sūtras enumerate the *tattvas* — *bhagavat* (*Tattva-Sūtra* sūtras 1–4), *jīva* (*taṭasthā-śakti*) (sūtras 5–7), *māyā* (*bahiraṅgā-śakti*) (sūtras 8–10), *svarūpa-śakti* (with its threefold *hlādinī*, *sandhinī*, *saṃvit*) (sūtras 11–14), and the eternal *līlā* (sūtras 21–24) — and articulate their relations under *acintya-bhedābheda*: simultaneous identity and distinction grounded in the *acintya-śakti* of Bhagavān (*Tattva-Sūtra* sūtras 15–20 with auto-gloss). The work serves as the Sanskrit doctrinal scaffolding the Bengali theological writings presuppose, intended to demonstrate to a learned pandit audience that the Caitanya school has a fully formed sūtra-grade doctrinal core continuous with the *Vedānta*-tradition (*Tattva-Sūtra*, opening *prayojana*-statement and concluding *upasaṃhāra*).

engaged_works.title: Vedanta-Adhikarana-Mala
engaged_works.summary: A summary of the *Brahma-Sūtra* organized by *adhikaraṇa* (topical clusters of sūtras), giving for each the Gauḍīya reading in the *Govinda-Bhāṣya* tradition of Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa (*Vedānta-Adhikaraṇa-Mālā*, Introduction, on its dependence on Baladeva's *Govinda-Bhāṣya*). Bhaktivinoda's purpose is to make the *acintya-bhedābheda* reading of the sūtras accessible at *adhikaraṇa*-resolution: each unit is paraphrased, the central *prameya* identified, and the Caitanya-school conclusion stated against the Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita alternatives (*Vedānta-Adhikaraṇa-Mālā*, *adhikaraṇa* entries on [BS 1.1.1](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1) *jijñāsādhikaraṇa*, 1.1.2 *janmādy-adhikaraṇa*, 2.1.14 *tad-ananyatva-adhikaraṇa*, and 4.4.1 *sampadyāvirbhāva-adhikaraṇa*). The work is the principal text in which Bhaktivinoda asserts the school's full standing as a *vedānta-darśana* with a coherent reading of every sūtra, not a sectarian devotional movement parasitic on others' philosophy. Together with the *Tattva-Sūtra* it constitutes the Sanskrit doctrinal armature behind the Bengali-language exposition of *Jaiva-Dharma*.

engaged_works.title: Bhagavatarka-Marichi-Mala
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed during Bhaktivinoda's mature period of doctrinal output.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored anthology of Bhāgavata-verses with commentary.
engaged_works.summary: An anthology (*mālā*, garland) of selected verses from the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* — the *marīcis* (rays) of the *Bhāgavata-arka* (Bhāgavata-sun) — organized by doctrinal topic and accompanied by Bhaktivinoda's own Sanskrit gloss (*Bhāgavatārka-Marīci-Mālā*, prefatory verses). The work serves as a topical concordance to the *Bhāgavata* for Gauḍīya doctrinal use: under each topical heading (*bhagavat-tattva*, with verses centered on *Bhāg.* 1.2.11 and 1.3.28; *jīva-tattva*, with verses from *Bhāg.* 11.2.37 and 7.5.23–24; *śakti*-doctrine, with *Bhāg.* 8.5.26 and 11.24.1–17; *līlā*-tattva, with *Bhāg.* 10.21–33; *sādhana-bhakti*, with *Bhāg.* 1.2.6–14; *prema*, with *Bhāg.* 10.29–33 and 11.6) the relevant *Bhāgavata* verses are gathered with concise interpretive notes establishing the *acintya-bhedābheda* reading. The work is the sectarian successor to Sanātana Gosvāmī's *Bṛhad-Bhāgavatāmṛta* and Jīva Gosvāmī's *Krama-Sandarbha*, supplying a more compact and pedagogically focused tool for the modern Gauḍīya scholar-devotee. It demonstrates Bhaktivinoda's command of Sanskrit doctrinal exegesis (alongside his more famous Bengali theological writing) and his programmatic insistence that the Caitanya school is fully grounded in *Bhāgavata*-authority. The selection of verses is itself an interpretive act — fixing the school's *Bhāgavata*-canon in the modern period.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Viveka
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Published 1893; one of Bhaktivinoda's mid-career systematic treatises.
engaged_works.summary: A Bengali philosophical primer in 48 verses with the author's own prose commentary, structured as an analytic discrimination (*viveka*) of the *tattvas*: *bhagavat-tattva* (*Tattva-Viveka* vv. 1–16), *jīva-tattva* (vv. 17–32), and *māyā-tattva* (vv. 33–48), with their internal articulations. The work engages directly with the philosophical alternatives current in late-nineteenth-century Bengal — Brahmo-Samāja impersonalism, Advaita-derived monism, and the various reformist accommodations to Western theism — and argues that Caitanya's *acintya-bhedābheda* alone provides a coherent philosophical theology adequate to both rational scrutiny and the data of devotional experience (*Tattva-Viveka* vv. 1–10 with auto-gloss, polemical engagement with Brahmo-Samāja and Western theism). Distinctive features: a careful articulation of the *acintya* as a positive epistemic category (the proper object of *adhokṣaja-jñāna*, neither contradictory nor reducible to ordinary rational determination) (*Tattva-Viveka* vv. 11–16 with gloss); a defense of the eternal individuality of the *jīva* against monist absorption-models (vv. 17–32); and an early statement of Bhaktivinoda's programmatic claim that Caitanya's school is fully *vedānta-darśana* and fully modern (*Tattva-Viveka*, prefatory and concluding *upasaṃhāra*). The text is among the most accessible entry-points into Bhaktivinoda's mature theology and circulated widely among Bengali Vaiṣṇavas.

engaged_works.title: Sajjana-Toshani
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Founded 1881; Bhaktivinoda served as principal editor through approximately 1900.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored / edited; Bhaktivinoda founded and was the principal contributing editor of this Vaiṣṇava periodical.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus on the periodical's editorial history.

engaged_works.summary: The Bengali Vaiṣṇava periodical that Bhaktivinoda founded in 1881 and edited for approximately two decades — the principal organ of the modern Gauḍīya revival and the venue in which much of his theological output first appeared in serialized form (*Sajjana-Toṣaṇī* vols. 1–17, 1881–1900: portions of *Jaiva-Dharma* serialised vols. 5–10; doctrinal essays passim; polemical pieces against Brahmo-Samāja impersonalism and reformist Hinduism vols. 1–4; biographical materials on Caitanya and the Six Gosvāmīs vols. 6–12; Bengali translations of older Sanskrit Gauḍīya works vols. 8–15). The journal's title — "That which gives joy to the *sajjana* (the virtuous, the spiritually qualified)" — encodes its editorial program: not popular evangelism but the cultivation of a renewed scholarly-devotional Vaiṣṇavism among the Bengali educated classes (Bhaktivinoda, *Sajjana-Toṣaṇī* vol. 1 issue 1 [1881], editorial preface). The *Sajjana-Toṣaṇī* is the documentary witness to the public-intellectual dimension of Bhaktivinoda's mission and the principal source for tracking the doctrinal development of his thought in real time. Subsequent Gauḍīya periodicals — *Gauḍīya*, *Bhāgavat-patrikā*, and others edited by his son Bhaktisiddhānta Sarasvatī and later successors — are direct continuations of this editorial lineage.
